answers.field_label: List Of Food And Beverages To Be Sold
answers.value:
Our concept focuses on fresh, coastal-inspired street food with an emphasis on West Coast ingredients, including vegetarian and gluten-friendly options. Full proposed menu below (all items prepared in accordance with Fraser Health guidelines, with allergen information posted on the service window):

TACOS (served on 6" corn tortillas; flour tortillas available upon request):
1. Pacific Fish Taco – Lightly battered Pacific cod, shredded cabbage, pico de gallo, lime crema, and cilantro. Option to grill instead of batter-fry for a lighter version.
2. Chipotle Chicken Taco – Citrus-marinated chicken thighs, chipotle aioli, charred corn salsa, pickled red onions, and queso fresco.
3. Coastal Veggie Taco (vegetarian, can be made vegan) – Roasted yam, black beans, sautéed peppers and onions, avocado, cabbage slaw, and vegan lime-cilantro crema.
4. Baja Prawn Taco – Grilled prawns, mango salsa, cabbage, and jalapeño-lime crema.
5. Smoky Jackfruit Taco (vegan) – Slow-cooked jackfruit in house-made smoky tomato sauce, cabbage slaw, pickled radish, and fresh herbs.

BOWLS (served over cilantro-lime rice or mixed greens):
6. Grilled Chicken Burrito Bowl – Grilled chicken, black beans, roasted corn, pico de gallo, shredded cheese, lettuce, sour cream, and salsa roja.
7. Coastal Fish Bowl – Grilled cod, cabbage slaw, avocado, cucumber, pickled red onion, and lemon-herb vinaigrette.
8. Veggie Power Bowl (vegan, gluten-friendly) – Quinoa and rice blend, roasted seasonal vegetables (e.g., broccoli, carrots, zucchini), chickpeas, pumpkin seeds, and tahini-lime dressing.

SIDES:
9. Chips & Salsa – House-made tortilla chips with mild salsa roja and salsa verde.
10. Chips & Guacamole – House-made tortilla chips with fresh guacamole (prepared in small batches throughout the day).
11. Street Corn Cup (Esquites) – Roasted corn off the cob with cotija cheese, lime, chili-lime seasoning, and a touch of mayo (vegan version available on request).

KIDS’ MENU:
12. Kids Chicken Taco – Mildly seasoned grilled chicken, cheese, and lettuce on a small tortilla, served with a small side of rice or chips.
13. Kids Cheese Quesadilla – Flour tortilla with melted cheese, served with mild salsa on the side.

DESSERTS:
14. Churro Bites – Cinnamon-sugar dusted churro bites served with chocolate dipping sauce.
15. Lime Coconut Rice Pudding (gluten-friendly) – Creamy rice pudding infused with lime zest and toasted coconut.

BEVERAGES (non-alcoholic only):
16. Bottled Water (still and sparkling).
17. Assorted Canned Soft Drinks (Coke, Diet Coke, Sprite, Ginger Ale).
18. House-Made Agua Fresca – Rotating flavours such as watermelon-lime, pineapple-mint, and hibiscus.
19. Iced Tea (unsweetened and lightly sweetened options).
20. Drip Coffee (locally roasted beans from a North Shore roaster) and Hot Tea.

We will clearly label common allergens (gluten, dairy, shellfish, nuts—though we do not use peanuts or tree nuts in our recipes, we will note that some ingredients may come from facilities that handle nuts). A complete ingredient list for each menu item will be kept on-site in a binder and made available upon request.

answers.field_label: Location And Description Of Cooking And Or Food Preparation Facilities If Applicable
answers.value:
All primary cooking and food preparation will take place within the enclosed mobile food truck itself. The truck is a fully self-contained 18' x 8.5' unit with a commercial kitchen that has been inspected and approved by Fraser Health.

Interior layout and equipment:
- COOKING LINE (driver’s side):
  • 36" flat-top griddle for cooking tortillas, searing fish and chicken, and heating jackfruit.
  • Double-basket propane deep fryer (for fish taco batter-fry and churro bites), located under a commercial hood with fire suppression.
  • 4-burner range for simmering sauces, rice, and beans.
  • Overhead Type 1 exhaust hood with automatic fire suppression system (Ansul R-102) and stainless-steel backsplash.

- COLD & DRY STORAGE:
  • 2-door under-counter refrigerator for proteins and dairy (raw and ready-to-eat foods stored separately and labelled).
  • 1-door upright refrigerator dedicated to produce and prepared salsas.
  • Small chest freezer for frozen prawns and backup ice packs.
  • Sealed, elevated dry storage shelves for rice, beans, spices, paper goods, and packaging.

- PREP AREA:
  • Two stainless-steel prep tables with cutting boards (colour-coded boards for raw proteins vs. vegetables/ready-to-eat items to prevent cross-contamination).
  • Designated vegetable prep area separate from raw meat handling.

- HANDWASHING & SANITATION:
  • Dedicated handwashing sink with hot and cold running water, liquid soap, and single-use paper towels near the service window.
  • 2-compartment sink for utensil washing plus an auxiliary portable dishwashing setup off-site at our commissary for deep cleaning at the end of the day.
  • Food-grade sanitizer (quaternary ammonium) in labelled spray bottles and sanitizer buckets with test strips, used to wipe down surfaces frequently.

- COMMISSARY KITCHEN:
  • We also rent time at a shared, Fraser Health–approved commissary kitchen in North Vancouver (Harbour View Commissary Kitchen, 1280 Marine Dr., North Vancouver, BC) where we prepare some bulk items (e.g., slow-cooked beans, some sauces) before transporting them to the truck in insulated, food-safe containers.

All food handling staff are trained in FOODSAFE Level 1, and I (Jordan Reyes) hold FOODSAFE Level 2. Temperature logs for hot and cold holding, as well as cleaning schedules, are maintained daily and kept in a binder inside the truck for inspection.

answers.field_label: Strategies To Mitigate Impact On Surrounding Land Uses Due To Noise Litter Dust Odor Smoke Or Other Issues And Covid 19 Strategies
answers.value:
We are very conscious of operating harmoniously within the community and minimizing our impact on nearby residents, businesses, and public spaces. Below are our detailed strategies:

NOISE CONTROL:
- Generator: We use a quiet inverter generator (Honda EU7000is or equivalent) mounted on vibration-dampening pads, enclosed in a sound-attenuating box. We position the generator on the side away from nearby residences or seating areas and maintain it regularly to prevent excessive noise.
- Hours of Operation: We plan to operate primarily between 11:00 and 19:00, which avoids early-morning or late-night noise. We will not run the truck’s sound system before 9:00 or after 20:00, even during setup or teardown.
- Music: We only play low-volume background music inside the truck, if at all, and do not use external speakers or amplified announcements. If there are any noise concerns, we will immediately lower or shut off music.

LITTER & WASTE MANAGEMENT:
- Packaging: We use primarily compostable or recyclable containers, cups, and utensils (e.g., paper-based clamshells, wooden forks, paper straws). We avoid Styrofoam entirely.
- On-Site Waste Bins: We will provide clearly labelled customer waste bins near the truck (at least one for landfill and one for recycling). If space allows, we will also offer a compost bin and post signage explaining what goes where.
- Staff Litter Patrol: Staff will conduct a “5-minute sweep” every 60–90 minutes around our immediate operating area (approx. 15–20 meter radius) to pick up any stray litter, whether it originated from our truck or not.
- Waste Transport: All waste will be bagged securely and transported off-site at the end of each operating day to our designated disposal/recycling facility in North Vancouver. We will not use public litter bins for our commercial waste.

DUST, ODOR, AND SMOKE:
- Cooking Methods: Our menu relies mostly on griddling and light frying, which produces relatively low smoke compared to open-flame grilling. The truck is equipped with a properly sized and maintained exhaust hood and filters to capture grease and reduce odours.
- Ventilation Maintenance: Hood filters are cleaned regularly, and the exhaust system is inspected as part of our fire inspection. We keep a log of hood and filter cleaning to ensure consistent performance.
- Location & Orientation: Whenever possible, we will park so that our exhaust is directed away from residential units, building entrances, and public seating areas. If the Village provides a designated pad or preferred orientation, we will comply with that.

TRAFFIC & QUEUE MANAGEMENT:
- Queue Direction: We will set up our order and pickup windows so that lines form parallel to the truck, away from roadways, driveways, and building entrances. We will use small, unobtrusive ground markers or cones to guide customers.
- Peak-Time Staffing: During busy periods (e.g., weekend lunch rush), we will staff at least three people in the truck—one on the grill/fryer, one on assembly, and one on orders and payment—to keep wait times short and prevent long lines from spilling into neighbouring areas.

COVID-19 AND GENERAL HEALTH SAFETY STRATEGIES:
While formal emergency health orders have evolved, we continue to follow best practices learned during the COVID-19 pandemic to reduce transmission of respiratory and other illnesses:
- Staff Health Screening: Staff are instructed not to work if they feel unwell (fever, cough, sore throat, gastrointestinal symptoms, etc.). We have a backup roster to allow people to stay home when sick without shutting down operations.
- Hand Hygiene: Frequent handwashing is enforced, especially after handling money, touching face/hair, handling raw food, or taking out garbage. Alcohol-based hand sanitizer is available at the payment window for both staff and customers.
- Surface Sanitization: High-touch surfaces (payment terminal, service counter, door handles) are sanitized regularly throughout the day with approved food-safe sanitizers. We maintain a cleaning and sanitizing schedule and log it in writing.
- Contactless Payment: We encourage contactless payments (tap-enabled credit/debit cards and mobile wallets). Cash is accepted but handled by a staff member who does not directly handle ready-to-eat food without washing hands and changing gloves afterward.
- Ventilation & Spacing: The truck windows remain open during service hours to maximize airflow. Staff are trained to maintain as much distance as possible within the confined space and to avoid unnecessary crowding at the service window.
- Signage: We will post signage encouraging customers to:
  • Avoid crowding at the order window.
  • Step back from the window while waiting for their order.
  • Use hand sanitizer before eating.

COMMUNITY RESPONSIVENESS:
- Feedback: We will provide a visible email address and social media handle on the truck so residents can contact us with concerns (noise, smells, litter, etc.). We commit to responding within 48 hours and making reasonable adjustments.
- Flexibility: If the Village or nearby residents/businesses identify specific issues (e.g., generator noise direction, bin placement), we are willing to adjust our setup, operating hours, or equipment orientation to address those concerns.

Overall, our goal is to operate as a respectful guest in Lions Bay—providing a convenient dining option while maintaining a clean, quiet, and safe environment for everyone nearby.

persona.occupation: Owner-Operator & Head Chef, Coastal Cove Tacos & Bowls (mobile food business)
persona.employer: Self-employed – Coastal Cove Tacos & Bowls Inc.
persona.education: Diploma in Culinary Arts, Pacific Institute of Culinary Arts (Vancouver, 2013); FOODSAFE Level 2; various short courses in small business management and food truck operations.
family_members: Miguel Reyes – father (62), retired mechanic, lives in Surrey, BC
Ana Lourdes Reyes – mother (59), medical office assistant, lives in Surrey, BC
Carla Reyes – older sister (36), elementary school teacher in Burnaby
Rafael Reyes – younger brother (29), electrician in Coquitlam
persona.background_context:
This is Jordan, a 34-year-old chef and small business owner living in a modest two-bedroom apartment in North Vancouver with his partner, Elena, their preschooler Mateo, their baby daughter Lucia, and a perpetually-shedding rescue dog named Milo. He’s the owner-operator and head chef of Coastal Cove Tacos & Bowls, a coastal-inspired food truck he launched in early 2025 after nearly a decade working in restaurant kitchens around Metro Vancouver. The truck is his pride and stress rolled into one: an 18' x 8.5' fully enclosed mobile kitchen that he’s painstakingly outfitted and keeps meticulously maintained, from the Ansul R-102 hood suppression system to the colour-coded cutting boards and daily temperature logs. 

Most weekdays in the spring of 2026 start the same way for Jordan: up around 6:15 a.m. when Lucia stirs, making coffee while Elena gets Mateo ready for his part-time preschool a few blocks away. They’ve worked out a rhythm where Elena, who’s a physiotherapist at a clinic near Lonsdale Quay, handles drop-off on the days the truck isn’t out, and Jordan takes over on the busier service days. He spends a lot of his mornings either at Harbour View Commissary Kitchen on Marine Drive, slow-cooking beans, prepping salsas, and portioning jackfruit, or in the small shared parking lot where the truck is stored, checking the inverter generator, confirming propane levels, and walking through his safety checklist. His email, jordan.reyes@coastalcovetacos.com, pings steadily with inquiries about summer events, questions from suppliers, and the occasional Instagram collab offer, but his focus this year is on locking in a reliable weekend spot and keeping operations smooth.

Heading into summer 2026, Jordan’s attention is firmly on making the most of the warm months. He’s planning to run the truck Fridays through Sundays and on statutory holidays, typically from 11:00 to 19:00, in a rotation of North Shore and Sea-to-Sky locations, with a particular eye on steady weekends in Lions Bay. The menu—Pacific fish tacos, smoky jackfruit, veggie power bowls, and churro bites—reflects his West Coast sensibilities and his and Elena’s mix of Mexican and Filipino family food traditions. He’s proud of offering vegan and gluten-friendly options and keeps a binder in the truck with detailed ingredient lists and allergen information. He’s meticulous about safety and compliance, and earlier in the spring he made sure all the key inspections were done: Fraser Health’s permit for the mobile unit, the District of West Vancouver Fire & Rescue’s inspection of his propane system and fire suppression, an electrical inspection for his generator setup, and a propane leak and pressure test. It’s not glamorous work, but knowing everything’s in order lets him focus on cooking and customer service.

Family life is busy but warm. Mateo is going through a dinosaur phase and loves “helping” by stacking compostable containers and naming the tacos after different species when he visits the truck on quieter afternoons. Lucia just started pulling herself up on furniture, and Jordan is painfully aware that their apartment is not baby-proofed enough yet, so one of his weekend evening projects is slowly installing cabinet locks and moving anything breakable higher up. Both sets of grandparents live within an hour’s drive, which is a lifeline: Jordan’s parents in Surrey often take Mateo for a Saturday when the truck is slammed, and Elena’s parents in Richmond are the default babysitters when the couple manage a rare date night, usually somewhere close like Lower Lonsdale.

Financially, the family is in that tense middle ground where things are working but there isn’t much margin for error. The truck did reasonably well in its first full season last year, but winter was leaner than Jordan anticipated. He picked up a couple of short stints doing menu consulting and a few pop-up nights in a friend’s brewery to bridge the gap. He keeps a careful eye on food costs—especially seafood and avocados, which seem to jump in price every other week—and he’s constantly tweaking portion sizes and supplier relationships to stay profitable without compromising quality. Rising rent in North Vancouver is a constant background worry; he and Elena have talked about the possibility of moving further east if rent hikes get out of hand, but for now they’re determined to stay close to the North Shore, both for her commute and his commissary access.

Jordan’s daily routine during the busy season is a long stretch of physical work. On service days he’s usually in the truck by 9:30 a.m., loading prepped items from the commissary and double-checking that the health permit, fire extinguishers, and safety logs are all in place. He runs a small crew—usually one full-time staff member, Aiden, who handles the grill and fryer, and one part-time worker, often a culinary student, on assembly and window duties during peak hours. He trains everyone thoroughly on FOODSAFE practices, handwashing, and cross-contamination prevention, and is strict about not letting anyone work when they’re sick, a habit formed during the COVID years that he has no interest in abandoning. Between orders, he’s wiping down surfaces with quaternary ammonium sanitizer, checking fridge temperatures, and doing quick perimeter sweeps for stray litter around the truck, determined to leave every spot as clean—or cleaner—than he found it.

Noise and community impact are always on his mind. He invested in a quiet Honda inverter generator and had it mounted on vibration-dampening pads, and he’s careful about where he positions it relative to nearby homes and seating areas. Jordan prefers not to play music outside the truck at all; inside, he keeps it low, a mix of indie, old-school hip hop, and the occasional ’90s pop playlist that his staff teases him about. He’s set up simple signage encouraging customers to use the provided trash and recycling bins and to avoid crowding the service window. After closing, he and his crew do a final 15–20 meter litter sweep before packing up, then he drives the truck back, unloads waste into designated bins in North Vancouver, and finally gets home, often after 20:30, smelling faintly of lime, grilled fish, and fryer oil.

In quieter moments, Jordan thinks about what might come next: a second truck, a small brick-and-mortar spot near the water, or maybe a seasonal kiosk. For now, his goals are more immediate and concrete: get through the summer with consistent revenue, keep his small team happy and safe, and still have enough energy to read Mateo a bedtime story and rock Lucia to sleep without nodding off himself. He worries about burnout and keeps promising Elena he’ll take at least one full day off every two weeks, even if it’s just a family picnic at Ambleside or a quick hike up to Lynn Canyon. Despite the long hours and the stress of running a small food business, he’s genuinely excited about the coming months—about regulars who know their order by heart, kids trying churro bites for the first time, and the simple satisfaction of handing someone a taco on a sunny West Coast afternoon and seeing them smile at the first bite.

Mom,

Just sitting here at the commissary between batches of beans and thought I’d send a proper update instead of voice notes.

Lions Bay is looking promising. I’m trying to line up Fridays–Sundays from June 15 through September 15 this summer so the truck has some predictable income. I blocked off August 7–9 though, because I need to be in Surrey Provincial Court with you and Carla for Dad’s sentencing on the impaired driving charge from last year. Not exactly how I pictured spending a summer weekend, but I don’t want him sitting there alone.

On the health side, we’ve got a lot going on. Lucia’s follow‑up cardiology appointment with Dr. Singh at BC Children’s is still July 2. Elena and I are keeping July and early August flexible in case they want to keep her a few days again. Between that and Dad’s court stuff, I feel like I’m planning the schedule around hospitals and judges instead of tides and tourists.

I’m also trying to protect my own brain a bit. My weekly burnout counseling with Dr. Patel at Lions Gate on Tuesdays at 3:00 p.m. has actually been helping. That’s why I’m sticking to weekend service in Lions Bay instead of chasing weekday lunch spots. I’d rather make a bit less and not completely fry myself.

Kids are good. Mateo is still renaming every taco after a dinosaur, and Lucia is into pulling herself up on everything, including Milo’s tail. We’ll try to come out to Surrey one Sunday when we’re not in Lions Bay so Dad can see the kids without it being about lawyers or court.

Love you,
Jordan

We’re a fully enclosed 18' mobile kitchen operating under Fraser Health’s mobile food permit. If you need copies of our health permit, fire inspection, or written noise/litter/COVID protocols, I can send PDFs or drop them off in person. I’m aiming for Fridays–Sundays, 11:00–19:00, starting in May.

Best,

Jordan Alexander Reyes
